subject: ap-chemistry
title: Nitrogen Oxides: Equilibrium, Thermodynamics & Titration
stimulus:
A student investigates the reactions of nitrogen oxides. One of the reactions in the investigation requires an
equimolar mixture of NO(g) and NO

parts.prompt:
The particle-level representation of the equimolar mixture of NO(g) and NO
(g) in the flask at the
completion of the reaction between NO(g) and $O_2$(g) is shown below in the box on the right. In the box
below on the left, draw the particle-level representation of the reactantmixture of NO(g) and $O_2$(g) that
would yield the product mixture shown in the box on the right. In your drawing, represent oxygen atoms
and nitrogen atoms as indicated below.
The student reads in a reference text that NO(g) and NO
(g) will react as represented by the equation below.
Thermodynamic data for the reaction are given in the table below the equation.
NO(g) + NO (g) N $O_2$(g)
2 2 3
DH DS DG

−40.4 kJ/mol −138.5 J/(K ⋅ mol ) 0.87 kJ/mol
rxn rxn rxn
parts.scoring_notes: From the balanced equation 2 NO(g) + O$_2$(g) → 2 NO$_2$(g): to produce an equimolar mixture of NO and NO$_2$, the reaction must have gone partially to completion. If 1 NO$_2$ and 1 NO remain, then 1 NO$_2$ was made (requiring 1 NO and 0.5 O$_2$ consumed). The reactant box should show 2 NO and 1 O$_2$ particles (or any consistent ratio of 2 NO : 1 O$_2$).

parts.prompt:
The student begins with an equimolar mixture of NO(g) and NO
(g) in a rigid reaction vessel and the
mixture reaches equilibrium at 298 K.
(i) Calculate the value of the equilibrium constant, K, for the reaction at 298 K.
(ii) If both P and P in the vessel are initially 1.0 atm, will P at equilibrium be equal to
NO NO N $O_2$ 2 3
1.0 atm? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (b)(i): Reaction: NO(g) + NO$_2$(g) ⇌ N$_2$O$_3$(g). ΔG° = 0.87 kJ/mol = 870 J/mol. $\Delta G° = -RT\ln K$: $\ln K = -\Delta G°/(RT) = -870/(8.314 \times 298) = -0.351$. $K = e^{-0.351} = 0.704 \approx 0.70$. Part (b)(ii): No, P(N$_2$O$_3$) ≠ 1.0 atm at equilibrium. $K < 1$ means the reaction does not strongly favor products. With initial pressures of 1.0 atm each, Q = P(N$_2$O$_3$)/(P(NO)·P(NO$_2$)) = 0/(1.0)(1.0) = 0 < K. The reaction proceeds forward but only to a small extent given K ≈ 0.70. P(N$_2$O$_3$) at equilibrium will be less than 1.0 atm.

scoring_points.description: For the correct K value calculated from ΔG° = −RT ln K: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$\Delta G° = 0.87\text{ kJ/mol} = 870\text{ J/mol}$
$K = e^{-\Delta G°/(RT)} = e^{-870/(8.314 \times 298)} = e^{-0.351} \approx 0.70$

wrong_examples: Using ΔH directly to find K without ΔG° — K is related to ΔG°, not ΔH°.
Forgetting to convert kJ to J — ΔG° = 870 J/mol (not 0.87 J/mol) for use with R = 8.314 J/(mol·K).
common_traps: Use consistent units: R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), ΔG° must be in J/mol. Convert 0.87 kJ/mol → 870 J/mol.

scoring_points.description: For 'No' with reasoning that K < 1 means limited product formation: 1 point

required_elements: No, P(N$_2$O$_3$) will not equal 1.0 atm at equilibrium.
K ≈ 0.70 < 1 — the equilibrium does not strongly favor product formation. The reaction proceeds forward to a limited extent.
alternatives.correct_example: No. K ≈ 0.70 < 1, meaning the reverse reaction is slightly favored. Starting with P(NO) = P(NO$_2$) = 1.0 atm, Q = 0 initially, so the reaction proceeds forward. However, because K < 1, only a fraction of reactants is converted to N$_2$O$_3$. P(N$_2$O$_3$) at equilibrium will be less than 1.0 atm.

wrong_examples: Saying P(N$_2$O$_3$) = 1.0 atm — with K < 1, the equilibrium mixture is mostly reactants.
Saying K > 1 leads to complete conversion — K = 0.70 < 1, not greater than 1.
common_traps: K < 1: products are NOT favored; at equilibrium, reactant concentrations/pressures exceed product. K > 1: products favored. K = 1: roughly equal.

parts.prompt:
The student hypothesizes that increasing the temperature will increase the amount of N
$O_2$(g) in the
equilibrium mixture. Indicate whether you agree or disagree with the hypothesis. Justify your answer.
N
$O_2$(g) reacts with water to form nitrous acid, HNO
(aq), a compound involved in the production of acid rain.
The reaction is represented below.
N $O_2$(g) + $H_2O$(l) → 2 HNO (aq)
2 3 2 2
parts.scoring_notes: Disagree. The reaction NO + NO$_2$ ⇌ N$_2$O$_3$ is exothermic (ΔH° = −40.4 kJ/mol). For an exothermic reaction, increasing temperature shifts the equilibrium to the LEFT (toward reactants, Le Chatelier's principle). The amount of N$_2$O$_3$ decreases, not increases.

scoring_points.description: For disagreeing with the correct Le Chatelier justification for an exothermic reaction: 1 point

required_elements: The reaction is exothermic (ΔH° = −40.4 kJ/mol). Increasing temperature shifts the equilibrium to the left (toward reactants). The amount of N$_2$O$_3$ decreases.
alternatives.correct_example: Disagree. The forward reaction is exothermic (ΔH° = −40.4 kJ/mol). By Le Chatelier's principle, increasing the temperature is equivalent to adding heat to an exothermic reaction, which shifts the equilibrium toward the reactants (left). This decreases the amount of N$_2$O$_3$(g) in the equilibrium mixture.

wrong_examples: Agreeing — increasing temperature shifts equilibrium toward the endothermic direction (left for this exothermic reaction), reducing product.
Using ΔS to justify — temperature effects on K are governed by ΔH, not ΔS directly.
common_traps: Le Chatelier and temperature: exothermic reaction (ΔH < 0) → increasing T shifts equilibrium LEFT (less product). Endothermic reaction (ΔH > 0) → increasing T shifts equilibrium RIGHT (more product).

parts.prompt:
The skeletal structure of the HNO
molecule is shown in the box below.
(i) Complete the Lewis electron-dot diagram of the HNO
molecule in the box below, including any lone
pairs of electrons.
$H_2O$ N O
(ii) Based on your completed diagram above, identify the hybridization of the nitrogen atom in the HNO
molecule.
To produce an aqueous solution of HNO
, the student bubbles N
$O_2$(g) into distilled water. Assume that the
reaction goes to completion and that HNO
is the only species produced. To determine the concentration of
HNO
(aq) in the resulting solution, the student titrates a 100. mL sample of the solution with 0.100 M KOH(aq).
The neutralization reaction is represented below.
HNO (aq) + $OH^-$ (aq) → NO − (aq) + $H_2O$(l)
2 2 2
The following titration curve shows the change in pH of the solution during the titration.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (d)(i): HNO$_2$ Lewis structure: H−O−N=O. H bonded to one O, which is bonded to N, with a double bond to the second O. Lone pairs: each terminal O has 2 lone pairs; the central O (bonded to H and N) has 2 lone pairs. Total valence electrons = 1+6+5+6 = 18. Part (d)(ii): In HNO$_2$, the nitrogen atom has 2 bonding groups (double bond to O and single bond to O) and 1 lone pair = 3 electron domains → sp$^2$ hybridized.

parts.prompt:
Use the titration curve and the information above to
(i) determine the initial concentration of the HNO
(aq) solution
(ii) estimate the value of $pK_a$
for HNO
(aq)
parts.scoring_notes: Part (e)(i): From the titration curve, the equivalence point is at approximately 30.0 mL of 0.100 M KOH added. Moles KOH = (0.100 M)(0.0300 L) = 3.00 × 10$^{-3}$ mol = moles HNO$_2$. [HNO$_2$] = 3.00 × 10$^{-3}$ mol / 0.100 L = 0.0300 M. Part (e)(ii): pK$_a$ is read from the titration curve at the half-equivalence point (where pH = pK$_a$). At V(KOH) = 15.0 mL (half of 30.0 mL), pH ≈ 3.4. So pK$_a$ ≈ 3.4.

scoring_points.description: For the correct initial concentration of HNO$_2$ from the titration curve: 1 point

required_elements: Read the equivalence point volume from the curve (approximately 30.0 mL KOH).
alternatives.correct_example: Equivalence point from curve: ~30.0 mL KOH added. Moles KOH = moles HNO$_2$ = (0.100)(0.030) = 3.00 × 10$^{-3}$ mol. [HNO$_2$] = 3.00 × 10$^{-3}$ mol / 0.100 L = 0.0300 M.

wrong_examples: Not dividing by the volume of the acid sample (100 mL) — moles of acid / volume of acid = concentration.
Using the half-equivalence point volume as the equivalence volume — find the inflection point (steepest rise) for the equivalence point.
common_traps: Equivalence point = the volume at the steepest slope of the titration curve (inflection point). Half-equivalence point = halfway to equivalence volume.

scoring_points.description: For estimating pK$_a$ ≈ 3.4 from the half-equivalence point on the titration curve: 1 point

required_elements: pK$_a$ is read at the half-equivalence point (where V = V$_{eq}$/2 = ~15.0 mL).
At V(KOH) = 15 mL, pH ≈ 3.4 from the graph. pK$_a$ ≈ 3.4.
alternatives.correct_example: At the half-equivalence point (15.0 mL KOH added), [HNO$_2$] = [NO$_2^-$] and Henderson-Hasselbalch gives pH = pK$_a$. From the curve at V = 15.0 mL, pH ≈ 3.4. Therefore pK$_a$ ≈ 3.4.

wrong_examples: Reading pK$_a$ from the equivalence point pH — pK$_a$ is read at the HALF-equivalence point, not the equivalence point.
Saying pK$_a$ = 7 — weak acid equivalence point is above 7, but pK$_a$ is read before the equivalence.
common_traps: Half-equivalence point: pH = pK$_a$. This is where exactly half the acid has been neutralized, so [HA] = [A$^-$] and log([A$^-$]/[HA]) = 0.

parts.prompt:
During the titration, after a volume of 15 mL of 0.100 M KOH(aq) has been added, which species,
−
HNO
(aq) or NO
(aq), is present at a higher concentration in the solution? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: At 15 mL KOH added (half-equivalence point), [HNO$_2$] = [NO$_2^-$] exactly (pH = pK$_a$). But the question asks after 15 mL — interpret as: equivalence volume is 30 mL, so at V = 15 mL we ARE at the half-equivalence point, meaning equal concentrations. However, if the equivalence volume from the curve is slightly less than 30 mL, 15 mL might be past the half-equivalence. In any case, at the half-equivalence point both are equal; past it, NO$_2^-$ predominates. The intended answer is: past the half-equivalence point → NO$_2^-$ is higher. [Note: if equivalence point is at ~30 mL, then 15 mL = exactly half-equivalence → equal. The College Board answer is HNO$_2$ is higher if the equivalence is at a larger volume, meaning 15 mL is BEFORE half-equivalence — answer depends on where equivalence is read. Typical scoring: if equivalence ≈ 30 mL → at 15 mL = half-equivalence → equal concentrations OR HNO$_2$ slightly > NO$_2^-$ if 15 mL is just before half-equivalence. Accept HNO$_2$ is higher with justification that 15 mL is before the half-equivalence point.]

scoring_points.description: For the correct species identification with Henderson-Hasselbalch reasoning consistent with the titration curve: 1 point

required_elements: Correct species stated (either HNO$_2$ if before half-equiv, or equal if at half-equiv).
Justification using half-equivalence point reasoning: pH < pK$_a$ → more HNO$_2$; pH > pK$_a$ → more NO$_2^-$; pH = pK$_a$ → equal.
alternatives.correct_example: If equivalence point is at ~30 mL, then 15 mL = half-equivalence point. At half-equivalence, [HNO$_2$] = [NO$_2^-$] exactly (pH = pK$_a$). Alternatively, if the equivalence point from the student's curve is greater than 30 mL, 15 mL is before the half-equivalence point and HNO$_2$ is at higher concentration (more acid remains than conjugate base).

wrong_examples: Saying KOH is present at higher concentration — KOH is the titrant and reacts with HNO$_2$; it is not a species in the solution being analyzed.
Ignoring the titration curve and assuming equal concentrations without justification.
common_traps: Henderson-Hasselbalch: if current pH < pK$_a$ → [HA] > [A$^-$]; if pH > pK$_a$ → [A$^-$] > [HA]; if pH = pK$_a$ → [HA] = [A$^-$]. Use the titration curve to locate the pH at 15 mL relative to pK$_a$.
